coredata.dc:title: A fast, sensitive and stable fluorescent fiber-optic chemosensor for quantitative detection of Fe3+ in real water and HepG2 living cells
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: He, Yan-Hui
Lai, Jia-Ping
Sun, Hui
Chen, Zhi-Min
Lan, Sheng
coredata.dc:description: Based on the fluorescence quenching mechanism of Fe3+ to moxifloxacin (MOX), a fluorescence probe has been found and exhibits high sensitivity, unique selectivity and good stability toward Fe3+. Moreover, the MOX probe can rapidly response to the change of Fe3+ concentration in pure aqueous solution. The MOX probe was utilized for quantitative determination of Fe3+ in real water sample, showing a good linear relationship (R =0.9980) between (F0–F) and [Fe3+]1/2 at the Fe3+ concentration from 0.05–15μM. The detection limit was found to be 0.032μM, which is much lower than that of most literature reported. Furthermore, by merging the MOX probe and a Y-tape fiber-optic spectrometer, a fluorescent fiber-optic chemosensor has been developed to quantitatively monitor Fe3+ in HepG2 living cells in vivo. The monitoring results are consistence with that obtained from the confocal imaging technique. The established method possesses the advantages of fast responsive, high sensitivity, unique selectivity, very stability and good repeatability for quantitative monitoring Fe3+ in real water and HepG2 living cells. In addition, the complex mechanism of MOX toward Fe3+ has also been explored by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and mass spectra (MS) in the present work.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence probe
Fluorescent fiber-optic chemosensor
Iron ion(III)
Moxifloxacin
Living cells imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The operation of fiber-optic spectrometer for fluorescence monitoring
Fluorescence characterization and selectivity of MOX toward Fe3+
Rapid monitoring Fe3+ on-line or on-site
Quantitative determination of Fe3+ in real water sample
Imaging and quantitative detection of Fe3+ in HepG2 cells
Fluorescence quenching mechanism of MOX toward Fe3+

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21107018 , 21477026 ), Science and Technology Key Project of Ministry of Education (212129), Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province ( 2014A030313525 ). We also sincerely appreciate Prof. Zhou Jian-Hua in Sun Yat-sen University for cell imaging service.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The color change under sunlight and luminescence under UV lamp of MOX and metal ions ((a) color change of MOX (20μM) upon the addition of various metal ions (20μM) in water; (b) the luminescence change of the mixture of MOX (20μM) and various metal ions (20μM) under the UV lamp (λ =365nm). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The fluorescence characterization of MOX and metal ions ((a) the fluorescence response of MOX (10μM) by mixing with different metal ions (20μM) in water solution. (b) the fluorescence response of MOX (10μM) in the addition of Fe3+ and/or EDTA. (A) MOX solution; (A′) A+4μL Fe3+ (final concentration of Fe3+: 20μM). (B) A′+4μL EDTA solution. (B′) B+4μL Fe3+. (C) B′+4μL Fe3+ solution).

Competitive combining experiments of MOX toward Fe3+ ((a) The influence of single metal ions to the interaction between MOX and Fe3+; (b) The influence of mixed metal ions to the interaction between MOX and Fe3+).(For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

On-line monitoring process for (a) the reaction velocity of MOX and Fe3+ and (b) fluorescence titration (3mL MOX of concentration at 200μM was titrated with 30μL Fe3+ of concentration at 1mM, namely C Fe3+ ≈10μM).

Evaluation of the quantitative detection of Fe3+ in aqueous solution with MOX probe ((a) The plot showing the change of fluorescence emission intensity with the concentration of Fe3+, Inset: fluorescence wavelength scanning of MOX solution (10μM) upon addition of various equivalents of iron ions; (b) Relationship between the relative fluorescence intensity of MOX and [Fe3+]1/2 (λ Ex =295nm, λ Em =503nm, voltage=700V).
Fluorescence images of HepG2 cells treated with various concentrations of Fe3+ ((a–f) Cells incubated with 0, 10, 20, 40, 80 and 160μM Fe3+ after treated with 20μM MOX for 30min, respectively).

Comparison of (a) The relative fluorescence intensity of the confocal microscopy images calculated by Image-Pro Plus software and (b) The fluorescence intensity of the confocal microscopy images detected by fiber-optic spectrometer.
Investigation of fluorescence quenching mechanism of MOX toward Fe3+((a) FT-IR of MOX and MOX–Fe3+; (b) proposed mechanism of fluorescence quenching mechanism of MOX toward Fe3+).
Comparison of the detection limit, linearity range, detection media and response time for Fe3+ between the present method and those reported in literature.

head.ce:title: A fast, sensitive and stable fluorescent fiber-optic chemosensor for quantitative detection of Fe3+ in real water and HepG2 living cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry & Environment, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, Guangdong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry & Environment, South China Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environmental Science & Engineering, Guangzhou University, Guangzhou 510006, Guangdong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science & Engineering, Guangzhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Lab Nanophoton Functional Material & Devices, School of Information & Optoelectron Science & Engineering, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, Guangdong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Lab Nanophoton Functional Material & Devices, School of Information & Optoelectron Science & Engineering, South China Normal University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Based on the fluorescence quenching mechanism of Fe3+ to moxifloxacin (MOX), a fluorescence probe has been found and exhibits high sensitivity, unique selectivity and good stability toward Fe3+. Moreover, the MOX probe can rapidly response to the change of Fe3+ concentration in pure aqueous solution. The MOX probe was utilized for quantitative determination of Fe3+ in real water sample, showing a good linear relationship (R =0.9980) between (F0–F) and [Fe3+]1/2 at the Fe3+ concentration from 0.05–15μM. The detection limit was found to be 0.032μM, which is much lower than that of most literature reported. Furthermore, by merging the MOX probe and a Y-tape fiber-optic spectrometer, a fluorescent fiber-optic chemosensor has been developed to quantitatively monitor Fe3+ in HepG2 living cells in vivo. The monitoring results are consistence with that obtained from the confocal imaging technique. The established method possesses the advantages of fast responsive, high sensitivity, unique selectivity, very stability and good repeatability for quantitative monitoring Fe3+ in real water and HepG2 living cells. In addition, the complex mechanism of MOX toward Fe3+ has also been explored by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and mass spectra (MS) in the present work.

ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that Fe3+ is an essential ion for maintaining the life of organisms by exhibiting an obligatory role in many biochemical processes at cellular level. Numerous enzymes need to use Fe3+ as a catalyst for electron transfer, oxygen metabolism as well as RNA and DNA synthesis [1,2]. Its deficiency or excess may cause hypoferremia or hyperferremia, accordingly. Recently, iron was found to be another important limiting element for phytoplankton primary productivity besides the elements nitrogen, phosphorus and silicon [3,4]. Thus, developing a fast, stable, high sensitive, but simple method for the qualitative and quantitative monitoring of ferric ion in environmental water and cells is critical important to clinic, human health and environmental protection. To date, there exist many procedures for detection of iron or ferric ion, such as inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [5], atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [6], electro-chemical methods [7], colorimetry [8], flow injection [9] and fluorescent approaches [10,11]. Indeed, the classical methods such as ICP-MS and AAS have very high sensitivity and selectivity, however, those methods are costly, requiring a tedious sample pretreatment, and must be performed by well-trained personnel, so they are very difficult to be used for detection of ferric ion in vivo and on-site. In addition, those procedures are difficult to detect different valence state of iron without derivatization.

On the other hand, the fluorescence-based chemosensing holds remarkable advantages over other methods because of its high sensitivity, easy operation, as well as the low cost. Various fluorescent probes and sensors have been constructed recently for direct detection of Fe3+ [12–22]. However, there still exist several neck-bottle problems for some present fluorescent probes and sensors such as the harsh preparation conditions, poor hydrophilicity and long responsive time [23–29]. The above disadvantages have greatly hindered their extensive applications in real water samples and cells in vivo.

In present work, a constructive and interesting discovery was found: moxifloxacin (MOX), a famous antibacterial quinolones drug [30,31] with good water-solubility and strong fluorescence property, can be used as an eminent Fe3+-probe. The MOX based probe could produce very fast and stable response to Fe3+ in aqueous media, showing high sensitivity and unique selectivity against 17 other metal ions. Furthermore, the probe can be regenerated effectively, and reused with high repeatability. In this study, we further investigated in detail the fluorescence quenching mechanism of Fe3+ toward MOX by mean of Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and mass spectrometry (MS) procedures. Finally, a novel chemosensor, which can provide high sensitive, stable and fast response for Fe3+ in real sample, has been successfully developed by integrating a self-assembly fiber-optic fluorescence spectrometer and the MOX based probe. With the established method, we have monitored the reaction process of MOX and Fe3+ online in HepG2 cells, and the investigation on the metabolic process of Fe3+ in cells in vivo is under way in our lab.

Moxifloxacin (MOX, 99%) was purchased from Wuhan Dahua Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd (http://www.whdhwy.cn/). Aqueous solutions of metal ions, including Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Be2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+ and Zn2+, were prepared from their nitrate or chloride (guarantee reagent), which were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China, http://www. aladdin-reagent.com). Fetal bovine serum (FBS), PBS pH 7.4 basic, 0.25% Trypsin-EDTA and dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) basic were purchased from Gibco (Shanghai, China, http://www.lifetechnologies.com/gibco). Paraformaldehyde fixed fluid (4%) was provided by Boster, Wuhan. Ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) and other reagents used were of analytical grade, and distilled water was used throughout the experiment.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded with a Hitachi FL-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) and a self-assembly fiber-optic spectrometer (Ocean optics USB2000+). The IR spectra were obtained using a FT-IR (Nicolet 6700, USA). Cell imaging was pictured by inverted research microscope (Nikon eclipse Ti, Japen). AAS and ICP-AES were purchased from ThermoFisher, IRIS Intrepid II XSP, America.

The human hepatocellular liver carcinoma cell line (HepG2) was provided by the Animal Experimental Center of Sun Yat-sen University. Cells were plated on 18mm glass cover slips and allowed to adhere for 24h and then cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and antibiotics (100mg/mL streptomycin and 100U/mL penicillin) at 37°C in the humidified atmosphere under an atmosphere of 5% CO2.

Generally, 0.0401g MOX was dissolved in 100mL distilled water to get a concentration of 1mM MOX aqueous solution as the stock solution for subsequent use. All the concentrations of metal ions and EDTA stock solution were 10mM. For selectivity and interference investigation, the final concentration of MOX and 18 different metal ions were 10μM (MOX) and 20μM (metal ions) in the mixed solution. Then the fluorescence of the mixtures was determined immediately using a Hitachi FL-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The excitation wavelength of fluorescence spectrometry was set at 295nm and the voltage was 400V in the fluorescence test except for the quantitative determination (700V). For the reversibility test of MOX probe, 4μL Fe3+ stock solution (10mM) was added into 2mL MOX solution (10μM) to make the final concentration of Fe3+ to be 20μM. The fluorescence intensity of the mixture was monitored by FL-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer immediately. Then 4μL of EDTA solution was added to the above MOX–Fe3+ solution. The fluorescence intensity was detected again. The above operation was performed repetitively for several times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The operation of fiber-optic spectrometer for fluorescence monitoring
ce:section.ce:para: A self-fixed Y-tape fiber-optic spectrometer (LED source light: 365nm and 405nm) has been applied for real-time monitoring of the fluorescence changes during reaction. The excitation wavelength of fluorescence spectrometry was set at 365nm. The fiber-optic detector was perpendicular to the quartz cuvette to emit an exciting light and at the same time to receive the emitted fluorescent signal. All the experiments were carried out at room temperature and the fluorescent signals were recorded in real-time by a computer.

The cells cultured in DMEM were treated with 2μL of 10mM MOX (final concentration: 20μM) and incubated at 37°C for 30min. The treated cells were washed with PBS to remove the residual MOX on the surface of cell, and then dispersed in 2mL PBS. Different volume of 10mM Fe3+ solution (0μL, 2μL, 4μL, 8μL, 16μL) was added into the above solution to react for 15min. Cell imaging was pictured by inverted research microscope (Nikon eclipse Ti, Japen) and the blue channel window was chosen as a signal output. The relative fluorescence was calculated with an Image-Pro Plus software.

For FT-IR characterization, 0.0401g MOX was mixed with excessive FeCl3. And the mixture was ground in mortar and placed into vacuum drying oven for drying overnight (about 12h). Then MOX and the reaction products of MOX with FeCl3 were characterized by FT-IR (NICOLET 6700) and MS (LCQ Deca XP MAX).

Zhujiang River water, Yanhu Lake water and tap water were collected in Guangzhou. The water samples were filtered 2 times through 0.22μm membrane filter before use and then analyzed under the optimal conditions by FAAS and ICP-OES. Standard concentration curve of iron ion was 0ppb, 11.2ppb, 22.4ppb, 44.8ppb and 100ppb, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence characterization and selectivity of MOX toward Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: In the preliminary experiment, it was found that no obvious color change can be observed when MOX solution was mixed with 17 metal ions of Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Be2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, K+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+ and Zn2+, respectively under natural light. However, the color of the MOX solution changed immediately to brown due to the mixing of MOX and Fe3+ (Fig. 1a). This experimental result promoted us to further investigate the fluorescent properties of MOX. As expected, only the mixture of MOX and Fe3+ displayed the phenomena of fluorescence quench under the illumination of UV light (Fig. 1b). Subsequently, the fluorescence properties of the mixtures of MOX solution (10μM) and various metal ions (20μM) were further investigated by fluorescence spectrophotometer. It was found that the emission peak of MOX appeared around 500nm under excitation wavelength of 295 or 365nm (Fig. S1). In the subsequent fluorescence investigation, 295 and 365nm were chose as the excitation wavelength for FL-2500 fluorescence spectrometry and self-fixed florescence optic-fiber spectrometer, respectively. As can be seen from Fig. 2a, the addition of Fe3+ shall cause significant reduction for the fluorescence intensity of MOX solution, while minor fluorescence changes can be observed by the addition of other metal ions. The results indicate that MOX can response to Fe3+ by obvious fluorescence quenching with high specifity.

In the further investigation, another interesting phenomenon was found that above 90% fluorescence intensity could be recovered when EDTA (20μM) was add to the mixture of MOX (10μM) and Fe3+ (20μM) (Fig. 2b, line B) while the fluorescence intensity could be decreased again by the further addition of Fe3+ to the above solution (Fig. 2b, line B′). By repeating the addition of EDTA and Fe3+ in sequence, the increase/decrease of fluorescence intensity could be observed accordingly. The experimental results indicate that MOX can combine with Fe3+ immediately, and can be regenerated fast by simply treating with EDTA, accompanied by obvious fluorescence quenching/increasing. So, MOX is suitable to be explored for fast on-site detection of Fe3+.

To evaluate the stability of MOX and Fe3+-MOX, first, MOX was illuminated under 365nm for more than 60min using the LED source with the self-fixed optic-fiber spectrometer while the fluorescence intensity was recorded by the computer (Fig. S2). As can be seen from Fig. S2, no obvious fluorescence change of MOX could be observed under illuminating at 365nm for more than 60min. The result indicated clearly that no photobleaching could be observed for MOX, which suggested MOX is very stable. After that, the fluorescence intensity was reduced sharply as soon as 400μL of Fe3+ solution (final concentration: 400μM) was added into the MOX solution within 15s. Then the fluorescence signal was remained roughly at a stable level for more than 90min. These results indicated evidently that both of MOX and Fe3+–MOX are very stable under the test conditions. In addition, the influence of pH value of solution on the fluorescence of MOX was also investigated in detail. The result was shown in Fig. S3. As can be seen that the fluorescence intensity was affected slightly by pH value in pH<7 solution. The fluorescence intensity was slightly increased when the pH value of solution is above 7. This is possibly attributed that the structure of MOX is changed in basic solution. In this work, however, we involved the pH value of all solution is about 4–6 because Fe3+ will produce hydrolysis reaction when pH value is above 6.

The selectivity or anti-interference capacity is a very important factor to evaluate a fluorescent probe or sensor. It determines whether a probe or sensor can be applied in real samples with complicated matrix. To further investigate the selectivity of MOX to Fe3+ against other metal ions, 17 metal ions, including Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Be2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Hg2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+ and Zn2+ were mixed with MOX, respectively. As shown in Figs. 2a and 3a, the fluorescence intensities of these mixtures were almost as same as that of pure MOX solution with the relative average error of 3.37%. However, a severe fluorescence quenching has happened immediately with the addition of Fe3+ into the above mixture of MOX and the competing metal solution (Fig. 3a).

Meanwhile, in order to estimate the possibility to detect Fe3+ in real complicated samples with MOX, we also investigated the interference of the mixed 17 reference metal ions to the interaction between MOX and Fe3+. For this purpose, 17 reference metal ions were added into MOX aqueous solution (10μM) to make the concentration to be 20μM for each metal ion. As expected, the addition of the mixture of 17 metal ions did not affect the fluorescence of MOX solution (Fig. 3b, black line). In contrast, the fluorescence reduced greatly when Fe3+ solution was added into the above mixture (Fig. 3b, red line), which suggested that Fe3+ exhibits a high fluorescence quenching effect to MOX. Thus, it is concluded that MOX can be used as a high selective probe for the detection of Fe3+ without the interferences from other metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rapid monitoring Fe3+ on-line or on-site
ce:section.ce:para: For an on-site rapid detection method, the response time is another critical factor. Regrettably, most of the publications on fluorescent probes or sensors did not discuss on this point. In some literatures the reaction time is reported to be above 30min [23,25]. Apparently, these probes are not suitable for on-site detection due to their long responsive times. In order to monitor the reaction time of MOX and Fe3+ on-line, we applied a self-assembly fiber-optic spectrometry to monitor the fluorescence change during the reaction process. As can be seen from Fig. 4a, the fluorescence reduced quickly and achieved a stable value with 10s after adding Fe3+ solution into MOX, which suggested this complexation reaction was very fast under room temperature.

In addition, we have also monitored in real-time the fluorescence change of MOX with the addition of different concentration of Fe3+ solution (from 10μM to 400μM). The fluorescence of MOX was found to reduce quickly with the addition of Fe3+, exhibiting a classical quadratic curve relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of Fe3+ (Fig. 4b). Those results demonstrate that MOX is respected to be a fast responsive probe for monitoring Fe3+ on-line or on-site by integrating with fiber-optic spectrometry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative determination of Fe3+ in real water sample
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, in order to evaluate the stability and repeatability of the established method for quantitative determination of Fe3+, we repeated the test for 28 times in 3 days. Our results suggested the established method was very stable for quantitative determination of Fe3+ with RSD of 7.8% (Fig. S4). Meanwhile, in order to evaluate the accuracy of the proposed methods, we compared the detection results for the concentration of Fe3+ in tap water, lake water and river water (Zhujiang River, Guangzhou) with this procedure, FAAS and ICP-OES. Results showed that the concentration of Fe3+ in all water samples could not be detected by FAAS. However, the proposed method could detect Fe3+ in lake water to be 5.7ppb. Thus the proposed method is more sensitive than that of FAAS. It is noted that the concentration of iron in the same lake sample was detected to be 15.2ppb by ICP-OES. The different results is possibly ascribed to that only Fe3+ could be detected by the proposed procedure while the total iron with different speciation in water sample had contributed to the higher results of ICP-OES, which cannot be applied in the speciation analysis for metal ions. In order to further validate the accuracy of the established method, the recovery test was carried out by spiking 0.1μM Fe3+ into the above samples. The recoveries between 92 and 118% with RSD (n =3) under 7% indicated the proposed method exhibits good accuracy.

In a word, the established procedure is demonstrated to be a very fast, stable and accurate method for quantitative determination of Fe3+ in real water sample with high selectivity and sensitivity. Owing to these advantages of the present method, it is respect to construct a standard method for rapid determination of Fe3+ in water sample in real time by combining MOX chemosensor with fiber-optic spectrometry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging and quantitative detection of Fe3+ in HepG2 cells
ce:section.ce:para: In order to examine the ability of MOX to track the level of intracellular Fe3+, HepG2 cells were incubated with MOX (20μM) for 30min at 37°C and then washed with PBS 3 times to remove excess MOX [32]. The treated cells were incubated respectively with 0, 10, 20, 40, 80, 160μM Fe3+ for 15min, and then imaged by a confocal fluorescence microscope. As can be seen from Fig. 6 , the fluorescence intensity of the cell was gradually reduced with the increase of Fe3+ concentration. And the relative fluorescence intensities of the confocal microscopy images were evaluated by an Image-Pro Plus software (Fig. 7a). The result suggested that the MOX can be used for the quantitative determination of the cellular iron level change in HepG2 cells [14].

To verify the results calculated by the microscopy software, a self-assembly fluorescent fiber-optic chemosensor was used to monitor the fluorescence intensity in vivo on the cover slips which have been loaded with the incubated HepG2 cells. Fig. 7b showed the relationship between fluorescence intensity and Fe3+ concentration detected with fiber-optic chemosensor. It is worth noting that a very similar outline and trend could be observed between results from the two different methods. However, fiber-optic chemosensor can give better repeatability in detection with results showing lower RSD than the image method. In conclusion, the integration of the fluorescent probe MOX and the self-assembly fluorescent fiber-optic chemosensor can provide a very fast, sensitive, selective and accurate method for monitoring Fe3+ in living cells in vivo.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching mechanism of MOX toward Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the fluorescence quenching mechanism of MOX toward Fe3+, FT-IR and ESI-MS were applied to investigate the fluorescence quenching mechanism. Firstly, the infrared spectra of MOX and the complex MOX–Fe3+ were investigated, respectively. As can be seen in Fig. 8a, the CO stretching vibration at 1704cm−1 in MOX almost disappears in MOX–Fe3+. It implies the carboxyl group (CO) in MOX has been involved in the complex reaction with Fe3+. Moreover, the skeletal vibration peaks of the benzene ring at 1620cm−1, 1514 and 1452cm−1 are shifted to 1594, 1440 and 1357cm−1 in MOX–Fe3+, respectively. This result indicates the skeletal vibration energy of the benzene ring is changed after MOX complex with Fe3+. Meanwhile, the band of continuous double bond (CCC or CCO) at 2161cm−1 is observed in MOX–Fe3+. These changes indicate clearly that the benzene rings in MOX have been connected together with Fe3+ as a bridge in the complex MOX–Fe3+. Owing to the coordination between MOX and Fe3+, the electrons can transfer in a large domain, which account for the phenomena of the fluorescence quench of the MOX probe toward Fe3+.

To further investigate the molar ratio between MOX and Fe3+ for the binding reaction, we also characterized the mass spectra of MOX–Fe3+. As can be seen from Fig. S5, The mass spectra at peak m/z 797.71 could be identified as the molecular ion peak of [2(MOX–OCH3)–Fe3+], which indicated that the molecular ratio of MOX to Fe3+ is 2:1 (2MOX:Fe3+). Based on the above results, the schematic graph illustrating the coordination between MOX and Fe3+, as well as the fluorescence quenching mechanism of MOX toward Fe3+, are shown in Fig. 8b. It can be estimated that the conjugated electron cloud of MOX is destroyed, and the electrons can transfer in a large domain resulting in fluorescence quenching after the formation of the complex between MOX with Fe3+. Thus, this is a typical fluorescence quenching mechanism based on photo-induced electron transfer (PET).

In summary, MOX was used as a fluorescent probe for quantitative detection of Fe3+ based on the fluorescence quenching, showing properties such as fast responsive, high selective and sensitive, very stable and accurate. By combining of this fluorescent probe and fiber-optic spectrometry, a fast, sensitive, selective, accurate and simple procedure for detection of Fe3+ has been established. The proposed method has been used for conveniently monitoring of Fe3+ in real water and HepG2 cells in real-time and on-line. Thus, the established method is respect to be recommended as a standard method for rapid determination of Fe3+ in various samples such as environmental water, beverages and foods.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21107018, 21477026), Science and Technology Key Project of Ministry of Education (212129), Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (2014A030313525). We also sincerely appreciate Prof. Zhou Jian-Hua in Sun Yat-sen University for cell imaging service.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2015.11.048.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biological oceanography—complex lessons of iron uptake
The relative influences of nitrogen and phosphorus on oceanic primary production
Determination of trace Cd, Cu, Fe, Pb and Zn in diesel and gasoline by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry after sample clean up with hollow fiber solid phase microextraction system
Application of the extraction induced by emulsion breaking for the determination of Cu, Fe and Mn in used lubricating oils by flame atomic absorption spectrometry
Sensitive determination of iron(III) by gold electrode modified with 2-mercaptosuccinic acid self-assembled monolayer
A single thiourea-appended 1,8-naphthalimide chemosensor for three heavy metal ions: Fe3+, Pb2+, and Hg2+
Simple flow injection method for simultaneous spectrophotometric determination of Fe(II) and Fe(III)
Hybrid carbon source for producing nitrogen-doped polymer nanodots: one-pot hydrothermal synthesis, fluorescence enhancement and highly selective detection of Fe(III)
7-Oxy-3-(3,4,5-tri- methoxyphenyl)coumarin substituted phthalonitrile derivatives as fluorescent sensors for detection of Fe3+ ions: experimental and theoretical study
A simple, selective, fluorescent iron(III) sensing material based on peripheral carbazole
Sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots as a novel fluorescent probe for highly selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+
A novel fluorescent “turn-on” chemosensor for nanomolar detection of Fe(III) from aqueous solution and its application in living cells imaging
A dual colorimetric–ratiometric fluorescent probe NAP-3 for selective detection and imaging of endogenous labile iron(III) pools in C. elegans
Anthryl-1,2,4-oxadiazole-substituted calix[4]arenes as highly selective fluorescent chemodosimeters for Fe3+
A novel FRET ‘off-on’ fluorescent probe for the selective detection of Fe3+, Al3+ and Cr3+ ions: Its ultrafast energy transfer kinetics and application in live cell imaging
Synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for sensitive, label-free detection of Fe(III) in aqueous media
A highly selective and sensitive Fe3+ fluorescent sensor by assembling three 1,8-naphthalimide fluorophores with a tris(aminoethylamine) ligand
A N-stablization rhodamine-based fluorescent chemosensor for Fe3+ in aqueous solution and its application in bioimaging
A fluorescence ratiometric chemosensor for Fe3+ based on TBET and its application in living cells
A benzimidazole-based ratiometric fluorescent sensor for Cr3+ and Fe3+ in aqueous solution
A new selective fluorescent chemical sensor for Fe3+ based on rhodamine B and a 1,4,7,10-tetraoxa-13-azacyclopentadecane conjugate and its imaging in living cells
A new fluorescent sensor for the determination of Iron(III) in semi-aqueous solution
Four Rhodamine B-based fluorescent chemosensor for Fe3+ in aqueous solution
Glutathione capped silver nanoclusters-based fluorescent probe for highly sensitive detection of Fe3+
A Pyrene-based highly selective turn-on fluorescent chemosensor for iron(III) ions and its application in living cell imaging
Determination of iron(III) based on the fluorescence quenching of rhodamine B derivative
Fluorescent and chromogenic receptor bearing amine and hydroxyl functionality for iron(III) detection in aqueous solution
Impact of poor compliance with levofloxacin and moxifloxacin on respiratory tract infection antimicrobial efficacy: a pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic simulation study
Moxifloxacin versus ofloxacin plus metronidazole in uncomplicated pelvic inflammatory disease: results of a multicentre, double blind, randomised trial
New water-soluble highly selective fluorescent chemosensor for Fe(III) ions and its application to living cell imaging
A GBI@PPyNWs-based prototype of reusable fluorescence sensor for the detection of Fe3+ in aqueous solution
A novel rhodamine-based fluorescent and colorimetric “off-on” chemosensor and investigation of the recognizing behavior towards Fe3+

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jia-Ping Lai received his PhD degree in 2002 from Nankai University, P.R. China. He worked as a postdoctoral fellow in Institute of Hydrochemistry, Technical University of Munich, Germany (2002–2004) and as a research fellow in Department of Community, Occupational and Family Medicine, National University of Singapore, Singapore (2004–2006). Now he is a professor in School of Chemistry & Environment, South China Normal University, China. He is a member of International Association of Molecular Imprinting and Chinese Chemical Society. His current research interests mainly focus on developing the molecular imprinting technique, fluorescent probe and chemosensor as well as fiber-optic sensor for analysis of various targets such as medicine, pollutant and heavy metal ions in biological samples, environmental samples and food samples.

Yan-Hui He graduated from Longyan University, China in 2013. She is a Master student in School of Chemistry & Environment, South China Normal University. Her current research is centered on fluorescence probe, fiber-optic sensor and molecular imprinting technique.

Hui Sun received her PhD degree in 2006 from The University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong SAR, China. From 2006 to 2011, she worked as research associate for post-doctoral research work in the Department of Chemistry, The University of Hong Kong. She is currently associate professor in the College of Environmental Science & Engineering, Guangzhou University, P.R. China. Her research interests include chemical sensors and biosensors based on molecular imprinting technology, and microfluidic chip-capillary electrophoresis devices for environmental, biomedical, and food safety application.

Zhi-Min Chen graduated from South China Normal University, China in 2014. She is a Mater student in School of Information and Optoelectronic Science and Engineering, South China Normal University. Her current research is centered on photothermal therapy of gold nanoparticles.

Sheng Lan received the Bachelor of Science, master's and Ph.D. degrees in physics from Peking University, Beijing, China, in 1989, 1992, and 1995, respectively. He was a Post-Doctoral Fellow with the School of Electric Electronic and Engineering, Nanyang Technological University, Singapore. Later he joined the Materials Science Department, Tsukuba University, Tsukuba, Japan, as a Research Associate. Now he is a Professor in School of Information and Optoelectronic Science and Engineering, South China Normal University. He has published more than 150 scientific papers in peer reviewed international journals. He is a member of the Optical Society of America. His current research interests include nanophotonics, nonlinear and transient optics, and semiconductor materials and devices.
